method_atom: a multiscale modelling of a two-component system undergoing demixing and coarsening, where the growth of separated domains is coupled to a long-range interaction field generated by the geometric mismatch between the coexisting phases; depending on the sign and heterogeneity of the coupling this interaction can accelerate, slow, or arrest the coarsening, and when the coupling is directionally anisotropic it selects elongated domain shapes and regular periodic super-arrangements, with the response tunable by an externally imposed field. The machinery is formulated hierarchically as a sharp-interface (discontinuous boundary) description, a continuous-field (diffuse-interface) description, and a discrete-element description.
object_atom: a binary alloy separating into two coherent crystalline phases with differing lattice spacings and elastic moduli, whose precipitate morphology and spatial ordering respond to elastic anisotropy and externally applied mechanical stress.
brief_reason: The transferable part is coupling a coarsening two-phase system to a long-range, possibly anisotropic mismatch-induced interaction that reshapes and patterns the domains across sharp-interface/continuum/discrete scales; the alloy with lattice-mismatched elastic precipitates under stress is the materials-science-specific object.
